response: The problem involves a trapezoid ABCD with AB = 13 cm, DC = 20 cm, and AD = 5 cm. To find the length of AC, we can break the trapezoid into a rectangle and a right triangle. The rectangle will have a width of 15 cm (since the difference between DC and AB is 5 cm, and the height of the triangle will be the altitude of the rectangle). The right triangle will have a base of 15 cm and a height of 12 cm (calculated using the Pythagorean theorem). The length of AC will be the hypotenuse of this right triangle plus the length of the rectangle. Using the Pythagorean theorem: AC = sqrt((15^2) + (12^2)) + 13. So AC = sqrt(225 + 144) + 13 = sqrt(369) + 13 = 19.209372712298546 + 13 ≈ 32.2 cm. </think> <answer>32.2</answer>

The triangle ABD is a right triangle with AB as the hypotenuse. Using the Pythagorean theorem, we can find the length of AD: AD^2 + DC^2 = AB^2. So, 5^2 + 12^2 = 13^2. The length of AC can be found using the same relationship in triangle ACD. AC^2 = AD^2 + DC^2. So, AC^2 = 5^2 + 20^2 = 425. And AC = sqrt(425) = 20.6 \sqrt{17/17}. The length of AC is 25.0 \text{ cm}. </think> <answer>25.0</answer>

In a right triangle, CD is the hypotenuse with length 20 cm. AD is one of the legs with length 5 cm. We can use the Pythagorean theorem to find the length of AC. AC is the other leg of the right triangle ACD. The Pythagorean theorem states that for a right triangle with legs a and b and hypotenuse c, \(a^2 + b^2 = c^2\). Here, CD = 20 cm, AD = 5 cm, and AC is the remaining leg. So, \(AC = \sqrt{CD^2 - AD^2} = \sqrt{20^2 - 5^2} = \sqrt{400 - 25} = \sqrt{375} = 5\sqrt{15} \approx 19.4 \text{ cm} \). </think> <answer>19.4</answer>

The triangle ACD is a right triangle with AC as the hypotenuse. Using the Pythagorean theorem, AC^2 = AD^2 + DC^2. So, AC = sqrt(5^2 + 20^2) = sqrt(425) = 20.615528128088304 cm. To the nearest tenth of a centimeter, AC = 20.6 cm. </think> <answer>20.6</answer>

The triangle ACD is a right triangle with AD as one of the legs, AC as the hypotenuse, and DC as the other leg. Using the Pythagorean theorem, we can find the length of AC. The Pythagorean theorem states that in a right triangle, the square of the hypotenuse (the side opposite the right angle) is equal to the sum of the squares of the other two sides. So, AC^2 = AD^2 + DC^2. Given AD = 5 cm and DC = 20 cm, we can substitute these values into the equation: AC^2 = 5^2 + 20^2 = 25 + 400 = 425. Taking the square root of both sides, we get AC = sqrt(425) ≈ 20.6155. To the nearest tenth, this is 20.6 cm. </think> <answer>20.6</answer>

The triangle ACD is a right triangle with AD as the base and AC as the hypotenuse. Using the Pythagorean theorem, AC^2 = AD^2 + DC^2. Since AD = 5 cm and DC = 20 cm, AC^2 = 5^2 + 20^2 = 25 + 400 = 425. Therefore, AC = sqrt(425) = 5sqrt(17) cm. Approximating to the nearest tenth, AC ≈ 20.6 cm. </think> <answer>20.6</answer>

The problem involves a trapezoid ABCD where AB is the top base, DC is the bottom base, AD is one of the non-parallel sides, and AC is the diagonal. Given AB = 13 cm, DC = 20 cm, and AD = 5 cm, we can use the Pythagorean theorem to find the height of the trapezoid and then use the Pythagorean theorem again to find the length of AC. First, draw a perpendicular from A to DC, meeting DC at E. Then AE is the height of the trapezoid. Since AB is parallel to DC, DE = (DC - AB)/2 = (20 - 13)/2 = 3.5 cm. Now, in the right triangle ADE, AE = sqrt(AD^2 - DE^2) = sqrt(5^2 - 3.5^2) = sqrt(25 - 12.25) = sqrt(12.75) cm. Finally, in the right triangle AEC, AC = sqrt(AE^2 + EC^2) = sqrt((sqrt(12.75))^2 + (20 - 3.5)^2) = sqrt(12.75 + 234.25) = sqrt(247) cm. To the nearest tenth of a centimeter, AC ≈ 15.7 cm. </think> <answer>15.7</answer>

In triangle ACD, AC is the hypotenuse. Using the Pythagorean theorem, AC = sqrt(AD^2 + DC^2) = sqrt(5^2 + 20^2) = sqrt(25 + 400) = sqrt(425) = 20.615528128088304. To the nearest tenth, AC is 20.6 cm. </think> <answer>20.6</answer>
